title: HSM Shield operator: staged key promotion + TLS signer + graceful Helm upgrade hooks
preview:
Problem / Context: Customers running Private deployments in air-gapped or regulated datacenters require safe, auditable HSM key rotations and certificate replacements that can be performed with zero-downtime and an automated rollback path. Existing tooling handled key promotion but lacked an orchestrated, Helm-native flow for staged promotion, a local TLS signing shim for offline environments, and Terraform primitives to bind policy and grants consistently across upgrades.

This PR introduces the 'HSM Shield' operator and several installer surface changes to provide: staged key promotion (canary -> staged -> promoted) with automatic traffic fencing, a TLS signing adapter that can run in air-gapped clusters and perform CSR approvals via a controlled HSM signing path, Helm post-upgrade/rollback hooks to ensure safe cert churn during chart upgrades, and a small Terraform module to bind operator IAM/KMS grants as part of the installer.

What I changed (summary):
- Added a new Kubernetes operator: ops/hsm-shield-operator (controller + CRDs) implementing staged promotion FSM, audit annotations, and reconciliation backoff policies.
- Introduced a lightweight TLS signer sidecar (images/hsm-tls-signer) that validates CSRs and proxies signing requests to an HSM enclave over mTLS; supports offline signing via queued signed artifacts.
- Helm chart updates: templates/hsm-shield/*.yaml with post-upgrade hooks that quiesce traffic, rotate certs in a controlled sequence, and run a validation job before lifting the quiesce. Hooks are idempotent and safe to re-run.
- Terraform: modules/installer/kms-binding module to create consistent binding resources (policy attachments, grant objects) required by the operator in supported clouds. Includes tests for idempotency and drift detection.
- Migration script: scripts/migrate-sealed-secrets-to-protobuf.sh to transition sealed-secrets secrets to the new encrypted envelope format consumed by the operator.
- Docs: docs/private/hsm-shield-playbook.md with runbook instructions for canary promotion, rollback, and emergency key freeze.

Why this approach: The operator centralizes state (canary/promote/rollback) and emits audit-friendly annotations for KMS/HSM actions so customers can satisfy compliance requests. The TLS signer addresses air-gapped clusters by allowing sign operations to either happen synchronized with a physically-attached HSM or via an approved offline signing flow. Embedding Helm hooks lets cluster operators upgrade the installer without manual certificate choreography.

Testing and validation:
- Unit tests for operator FSM and reconcile loops included.
- Integration tests: e2e scenario that runs in CI using a software HSM shim (softHSM) to validate key promotion and TLS rotation flow. One e2e test is flaky under heavy load and was re-run in CI (now stabilized).
- Terraform acceptance tests validate binding creation and idempotency.

Rollout notes / migration:
- Operators will detect existing sealed-secrets payloads and create a migration job; the migration is opt-in by setting values.hsmShield.migrate=true in the values file.
- Default behavior is conservative: new operator will not auto-promote keys unless explicitly triggered by a PromotionRequest CR.

Checklist:
- [x] CRD definitions and RBAC
- [x] Operator reconcile + unit tests
- [x] TLS signer sidecar + image build scripts
- [x] Helm hooks and chart linting
- [x] Terraform binding module + acceptance tests
- [x] Migration script and docs

Related work: ENG-4127 (staged-rotation-spec), ENG-4189 (installer-terraform-binding), SUP-8421 incident follow-up automation.

Notes for reviewers: focus on reconcile idempotency, Helm hook safety (double-run behavior), and Terraform grant lifecycle. The operator logs include structured events to simplify incident replay.

title: staged-egress-nat-allocator-and-conditional-dns-delegation-rollback
preview: Motivation: customer on-prem control planes need deterministic AZ-aware egress NAT allocation and an auditable, reversible handoff of Route53 private delegation for cross-account resolver setups. This PR introduces a Terraform module and installer hooks to provision a staged egress NAT allocator with zone affinity, ephemeral ENI support for control plane endpoints, and a conditional DNS delegation workflow that can be rolled back safely. Summary of changes: 1) terraform/modules/egress-nat-allocator: new module that computes AZ affinity, allocates NAT Gateways or ENI-based NAT proxies based on deployment flavor, and exposes a staged allocation plan for rollback. 2) terraform/modules/controlplane-subnet: add tags and ownership metadata to enable deterministic subnet selection by allocator. 3) installer/hooks/preflight and installer/hooks/post-rollout: add scripted handoff steps to create cross-account Route53 delegation records, sign delegation tokens, and optionally register fallback resolver rules. 4) helm/redwood-private/templates/daemonset-egress-proxy.yaml: add annotations for ephemeral-eni lifecycle hooks. 5) tooling/rollback/rollback-manager: add a lightweight Terraform wrapper that can revert staged NAT assignments and revoke delegation tokens in a controlled order. Checklist: [x] module implemented and unit-tested, [x] helm changes linted, [x] integration tests for staged rollout and rollback, [x] migration plan documented in release_notes. Migration notes: new TF state resources introduce a staged "allocation_plan" remote state key; operators must run `terraform apply -var=apply_stage=true` to commit the staged allocation. By default the installer runs in "staged" mode to allow verification before commit. Rollback instructions: use tooling/rollback/rollback-manager rollback --plan-id=<id> which will reverse ENI attachments, release allocated NAT IPs back to the pool, and remove delegation tokens while keeping controlplane subnets intact. Testing: ran full CI (terraform fmt, terraform validate, helm lint, unit tests) and a canary deployment in lab region eu-west-1. Observability: added metrics for allocation latency and delegation propagation in prometheus/metrics.yaml. Security: delegation tokens are KMS-encrypted and rotated on rollback. Performance: expected ~2x faster failover when using AZ-affine ENI proxies vs cold NAT Gateway recreation in multi-AZ failover scenarios. Related work: builds on ENG-1197 (subnet affinity tagging) and ENG-2589 (endpoint lifecycle hooks). Risks: cross-account IAM role propagation requires operator IAM permissions; documented preflight checks fail early if missing. This PR is intentionally conservative about automatic commit to avoid accidental DNS flip. Commits summary and changed files are provided below.

title: Add offline Helm OCI seeding and precompiled BPF-CNI module bundles for air-gapped kernels
preview: Motivation: Customers running Redwood Private in fully air-gapped datacenters reported two recurring issues: 1) inconsistent CNI installs when kernels differ from our build matrix, and 2) manual, error-prone steps to seed private OCI/Helm registries. This PR introduces an end-to-end offline-friendly flow: packaged, precompiled BPF-CNI kernel module bundles for common enterprise kernels plus a new OCI-Helm seeder sidecar utility that can be run from the airgap bootstrap container to atomically seed registries and charts. Summary of changes: packaged kernel module artifacts per distro (ubuntu20/22, rhel8/9, rocky, almalinux) with vmlinuz/headers checksums; an installer hook that probes target nodes and selects the closest-compatible module bundle; an offline Helm OCI seeder that pulls from provided artifact tarballs and imports images/charts into the local registry with optional signature verification; terraform module updates to create a registry seed job and a registry credential secret; Helm chart tweaks to surface device-mapper and module-loader knobs; and tests and preflight checks added to the installer preflight. Design notes: To avoid kernel compilation in customer environments we ship prebuilt, signed .ko bundles plus a fingerprint manifest. The installer probes uname -r and glibc/kernel headers and performs a checksum-based compatibility match; if no exact match exists, it falls back to a closest ABI-compatible bundle and inserts a graceful warning in the console output and audit log. The OCI seeder uses an idempotent import layer: tarball->verify->import->promote so retries are safe. title: installer: tenant-zone CMK placement + installer-signed-audit-envelopes
preview: Background: customers running private/on-prem deployments requested stronger guarantees that customer-managed keys (CMKs) are provisioned and used only within their configured data zone and that audit events emitted by the installer are tamper-evident for compliance audits. This PR adds installer-side enforcement of CMK placement (zone mapping) and an optional installer audit-envelope signer that produces a signed, immutable wrapper for audit batches so customers can retain a verifiable trail outside the control plane. Scope: - Preflight and onboarding validation for tenant->zone CMK mapping (installer preflight fails if CMK references a different region/zone) - Terraform + Helm changes to surface a tenant_kms_map var that binds tenant identifiers to allowed KMS zones - Installer KMS affinity controller that ensures API calls for key usage are routed only to allowed endpoints and rejects cross-zone CMK refs - An installer audit-proxy that signs audit batches with a tenant-scoped signing key (optionally backed by the customer's KMS) and writes signed envelopes to the configured audit sink - Migration helper: attempts safe rebind when an existing CMK is in an unexpected zone and emits a migration plan for operators - Feature flag: infra.install.kms_zone_enforce (default ON for new installs; existing installs are opt-in until migration is applied) Checklist: - [x] Preflight failing behavior implemented and unit-tested - [x] Terraform var + docs added - [x] Helm templates and chart values updated - [x] Audit envelope signer implemented with backward-compatible toggle - [x] Migration tool and a dry-run mode - [x] End-to-end smoke that runs in the CI private-installer job - [x] Security review (SEC-88) completed Design notes and motivation: preventing accidental cross-zone CMK usage reduces blast radius for data residency policies and is required by some customers' contractual controls. The signed audit envelopes provide an append-only proof for regulatory auditors without requiring the control plane to be fully trusted; operators can export these signed envelopes to their long-term retention stores. Implementation details: - installer/bin/preflight-check: new checks validate kms mapping and fail fast with suggested remediation commands - installer/terraform/kms_binding.tf: adds tenant_kms_map variable and validation rules; plan shows a migration recommendation if mismatched - helm/templates/kms-binding.yaml: renders Kubernetes secrets/configmaps that bind installer components to the allowed KMS endpoints - installer/cmd/kms-affinity-controller: light-weight controller that enforces runtime usage of mapped endpoints; rejects attempts to use unbound CMKs (returns 403 to caller) - installer/cmd/audit-signer: signs JSON-lines audit batches using PKCS#7 envelopes; verification helper included in installer/bin/verify-audit-envelope - charts/private-installer/values.schema.json: schema bump; new top-level keys under kms and audit documented - tests/ci/kms-affinity-smoke.sh and tests/unit/test_audit_signer.py added - docs/installer/kms-region-pin.md: operator facing doc and migration steps Rollout plan: - Canary: enable feature for a single internal tenant group in staging for 48h - Migration: run migration-tool dry-run across existing installs, notify operators of required action, then apply opt-in flag to migrate - Broad rollout: enable enforcement for new installs only for two weeks, then begin outreach to existing customers with an auto-migration path and operator-run command. Backout: toggling infra.install.kms_zone_enforce=false disables enforcement immediately; audit-signer can be disabled independently. Security considerations: the installer stores only a reference to the tenant signing key by default; signing may be delegated to customer CMKs via the KMS plugin. Access to signing keys is guarded by RBAC and the installer will log all signing operations as part of the audit envelope. Testing: unit tests added for preflight, controller rejection cases, and signer verification. CI: initial run flagged a flaky test that was hardened; final CI passed. Docs: new operator guide and migration playbook. Related: ENG-4012 (installer security hardening), SEC-88 (audit-envelope design).

title: add-transactional-tenant-seed-controller-and-quiesce-hook-values
preview: Context and motivation: Private installer customers have hit flaky tenant seed runs and race conditions during rolling upgrades when tenant bootstrap jobs are requeued or retried. This PR introduces a values-first approach that wires an opinionated, idempotent tenant-seed controller configuration and a pluggable quiesce-hook scaffold so operators can opt into safe quiesce semantics during upgrades. Goals: (1) Provide a values schema for transactional seed semantics (lease, idempotency, backoff profiles), (2) Add templated quiesce hook that can be enabled per-tenant or cluster-wide, (3) Surface init telemetry toggles so the console and observability stack can correlate seed progress across retries. Changes are behind feature gates and default to backward-compatible behavior. Checklist: - [x] values.schema.json for charts/redwood-private with new top-level keys: seed.transactional, seed.backoffProfiles, quiesce.enabled, quiesce.gracePeriod - [x] templates/init-seed-job.yaml: new controller-driven mode + legacy job compatibility flag - [x] templates/quiesce-hook.yaml: hook template with leader-elector and optional preflight image verification - [x] chart helpers: functions for merging tenant overlays and defaulting backoff profiles - [x] unit tests for values validation and helm template smoke tests - [x] documentation stub in docs/helm/values-tenant-seed.md Related work: ENG-1429 (tenant bootstrap reliability), SUP-7241 (customer incident). Implementation notes: the seed controller config uses a small JSON blob that is translated into annotations on the seed job (leases, opaque id) so existing runners that don't adopt the controller remain functional. The quiesce hook is a lightweight Job template that runs as a pre-upgrade hook when enabled; it performs an idempotent drain + wait-for-kv-ack step and writes a hook-state ConfigMap key to guard rollback/unwind flows. Migration guidance: omitted values will continue to use legacy behavior; enabling "seed.transactional.enabled" triggers the controller-mode which is additive and reversible. See release notes for operational considerations.

title: introduce-sealedsecret-snapshot-rotator-with-transit-rekey-and-helm-upgrade-hooks
preview: Context and motivation: Private deployments need an auditable, rollback-safe way to rotate secrets and keep recoverable backups of SealedSecrets when performing upgrades or rekey operations. Existing sealedsecret workflows at customers relied on ad-hoc scripts that are error-prone during multi-cluster upgrades and HSM/KMS key rotations. This PR introduces a controller and operational tooling that: 1) periodically snapshots SealedSecrets into a versioned encrypted archive, 2) stores snapshots in a user-configurable backend (S3-compatible or internal object store) encrypted with a transit KMS (Vault/AWS KMS) envelope, and 3) supports staged rekey/promotion with a safe rollback path. The design emphasizes minimal blast radius: snapshots are immutable objects with metadata linking to the KMS key version that encrypted them. Highlights and checklist: - New controller: sealedsecret-snapshot-rotator (daemonset-compatible, leader-elected) - Helm values: .sealedSnapshot.enabled, .sealedSnapshot.retention, .sealedSnapshot.backend, .sealedSnapshot.kms.transitKeyPath, .sealedSnapshot.preUpgrade.hookEnabled - Terraform: add module redwood_sealedsnapshot_kms_binding to create IAM/policies or Vault roles and bind them to installer service account - Upgrade hooks: pre-upgrade backup and post-upgrade verification hooks added to the Helm chart; hooks are idempotent and safe to re-run - Rekey workflow: a two-phase re-encrypt step that creates new snapshots with the new key while leaving previous snapshots intact; an emergency rollback command is provided to re-decrypt with prior key when necessary - Auditability: controller emits events and metrics; snapshots include manifest with resource names, namespace, SealedSecret controller version, and KMS key ID - Tests: e2e upgrade test that verifies snapshot + restore across chart version bump; unit tests for rekey logic - Docs: README and Upgrade playbook explaining operational steps and recovery procedures Checklist: [x] Controller implementation and unit tests [x] Helm chart changes + migration hooks [x] Terraform module for KMS binding and example usage [x] e2e test for upgrade + restore [x] Docs and runbook added What changed (high level commits): - implement snapshot rotator controller and reconciliation loop - add encrypt/decrypt using Vault transit and AWS KMS envelope support - add Helm chart templates for Job hooks and CronJob/DaemonSet config - add Terraform module and examples for IAM/Vault role bindings - e2e tests and CI workflow updates - docs: upgrade playbook, operator config reference Backwards compatibility notes: Controller is opt-in via Helm values (.sealedSnapshot.enabled=false by default). Existing SealedSecrets remain unchanged unless operator is enabled. Rekey operations require explicit CLI/workflow initiation by cluster operators to avoid accidental mass re-encryption. Security considerations: snapshots are encrypted with customer-provided transit key; we added KMS key ID binding in snapshot metadata and require RBAC to restrict snapshot read operations. Rollback safety ensures previous encrypted snapshots are kept until explicitly pruned. Related work: builds on ENG-2903 (sealedsecret lifecycle improvements) and incident notes from SUP-1289.

title: Add mixed-version cluster compatibility and stitched-restore plan for cross-version migrations
preview:
Motivation: Customers running private deployments often end up with mixed-version clusters during rolling upgrades, partial rollouts, or emergency hotfixes. Existing restore tooling assumes homogeneous cluster versions which leads to failed restores or irreversible schema changes. This PR introduces a lightweight compatibility shim plus a new 'stitched restore' workflow that can perform deterministic, reversible restores across mixed-version clusters without requiring full cluster-wide upgrades beforehand.

What's included: 1) compatibility shim: operator-side probe and per-node capability matrix (version_probe.go) that detects which nodes accept forward/backward compatible migrations and which require guarded operations. 2) stitched-restore executor: an orchestrator (stitched_restore.go) that constructs a deterministic sequence of migration and replay steps, splitting large migration sets into reversible micro-ops, and annotating each step with pre/post-checks. 3) Helm changes: new migration-job and restore-job templates with hooks to sequence jobs and respect node capability labels; values.yaml exposes feature flags (enableStitchedRestore, migrationConcurrencyLimit). 4) Terraform changes: inputs to surface cluster version skew and to provision a temporary 'restore coordinator' role. 5) Docs and runbooks: operator runbook with dry-run commands, how to interpret reconciliation annotations, and rollback guidance (installer/docs/*). 6) Tests: unit tests and an integration harness that exercises restores between v1.14 -> v1.16 and v1.16 -> v1.14 scenarios.

Design notes: - Stitched restore operates in three phases: probe, plan, execute. Probe collects node capabilities and current schema versions. Plan produces an idempotent, reversible plan where each migration is accompanied by a compensating reverse operation and data-shipping instructions for large backfills. Execute runs steps with pre/post-checks and optional snapshot checkpoints between critical steps. - Backups are "stitched" by annotating each snapshot with the migration state it was captured at; the executor will apply migrations in the exact recorded order needed to bring the cluster to the target state or to a safe rollback point. - The compatibility shim is intentionally minimal: it does not change model behavior, only reports capabilities and exposes a small API consumed by the executor.

Backward compatibility and safety: - Default is off: enableStitchedRestore is opt-in in values.yaml. - All migrations are run in a dry-run validation mode by default when invoked through the console or CLI; an explicit --apply flag is required. - Snapshots are mandatory before any apply step; the restore mechanism will refuse to proceed without a valid, recent backup. - The plan contains checkpoints; the operator will create logical checkpoints in object storage and record them in the cluster's restore CR for visibility.

Rollback strategy: - Each migration is paired with a reversible companion. If an operation is non-reversible (e.g., data-destructive drop), the planner will split it into safe phases and perform additional copies/backfills so the logical rollback remains possible. - The PR adds a 'rollback-preview' mode that simulates rollback steps and reports the delta without applying them.

Testing and validation: - CI added an integration pipeline that runs stitched-restore scenarios using the test harness in installer/tests/integration. The CI job composes two clusters (v1.14 & v1.16), runs a restoration from a snapshot taken on v1.14 into a mixed cluster, verifies schema integrity and application-level smoke tests, then attempts an automated rollback. - Local dry-run scripts are included (scripts/restore/dry_run_validator.sh) for SREs and support engineers.

Rollout and operational notes: - Feature gated; rollout should proceed with a canary tenant and the console flag enabled for a single region. - Recommend enabling migrationConcurrencyLimit=2 for initial runs to reduce live workload impact. - Operator exposes restore CR conditions: PlanReady, InProgress, CheckpointCreated, Completed, Failed. - Audit logs record each plan step and the checkpoint artifact ID.

Security: - The restore coordinator requires a scoped service account; Terraform module adds inputs to wire an optional KMS key for snapshot encryption. - All operator-to-storage interactions use signed URLs and short-lived credentials.

Open items / follow-ups: - Improve planner heuristics for large-table online backfills (ENG-2890). - Add metrics for step-level latency and per-step tokenization/IO (follow-up PR planned). - Add e2e tests for on-prem air-gapped restore scenarios (blocked on infra harness).

References: Linear ENG-2765, ENG-2890. Related runbooks and manual steps are in installer/docs.

title: hook-priority-scheduler-and-template-dependency-resolution
preview: Introduce a hook priority scheduler and deterministic template dependency resolution for the private installer Helm charts and hook jobs. Motivation: tenants with large numbers of tenant-scoped init hooks were occasionally triggering concurrent heavy migrations and image pulls that caused network/registry rate limits and prolonged upgrade windows. This PR adds (1) a scheduler that assigns hook execution windows and priority buckets to init hooks via values.yaml configuration, (2) deterministic template dependency graph resolution so hooks declared with 'requires' ordering are applied consistently across helm renders and upgrades, and (3) an optional init-rate-limiter sidecar controller integration that coordinates cross-tenant concurrency using a ConfigMap-backed lease. The change includes: - new values schema fields (hookGroups, priorityBuckets, templateDeps, rateLimiter) with validation in values.schema.json - Helm template helpers for rendering hook annotations, leader-elector labels and per-tenant retries - A CRD-compatible annotation convention so the private-installer can surface hook group statuses to the installer controller (note: does not introduce a new CRD in this PR; uses annotated ConfigMaps for compatibility) - Unit and integration tests for template rendering and scheduler policy - Migration notes to preserve previous installs and a small upgrade hook to mark existing non-annotated hook resources for controller adoption Backwards compatibility: defaults keep existing behavior (all hooks single group, FIFO within group). Operators that opt in get controlled ramping and deterministic ordering. See linked Linear tasks and in-PR checklist for rollout steps.
text: Introduce a hook priority scheduler and deterministic template dependency resolution for the private installer Helm charts and hook jobs. Motivation: tenants with large numbers of tenant-scoped init hooks were occasionally triggering concurrent heavy migrations and image pulls that caused network/registry rate limits and prolonged upgrade windows. This PR adds (1) a scheduler that assigns hook execution windows and priority buckets to init hooks via values.yaml configuration, (2) deterministic template dependency graph resolution so hooks declared with 'requires' ordering are applied consistently across helm renders and upgrades, and (3) an optional init-rate-limiter sidecar controller integration that coordinates cross-tenant concurrency using a ConfigMap-backed lease. The change includes: - new values schema fields (hookGroups, priorityBuckets, templateDeps, rateLimiter) with validation in values.schema.json - Helm template helpers for rendering hook annotations, leader-elector labels and per-tenant retries - A CRD-compatible annotation convention so the private-installer can surface hook group statuses to the installer controller (note: does not introduce a new CRD in this PR; uses annotated ConfigMaps for compatibility) - Unit and integration tests for template rendering and scheduler policy - Migration notes to preserve previous installs and a small upgrade hook to mark existing non-annotated hook resources for controller adoption Backwards compatibility: defaults keep existing behavior (all hooks single group, FIFO within group). Operators that opt in get controlled ramping and deterministic ordering. See linked Linear tasks and in-PR checklist for rollout steps.

title: add encrypted ENI bootstrap flow and deterministic DNS mirror rollback hooks
preview: Context: Customers deploying Private control planes in strict environments asked for stronger network-level guarantees: (1) control-plane ENIs must be provisioned encrypted at boot and bound to predictable IP allocations so firewall rules and egress policies can be pre-provisioned, and (2) DNS mirroring used during control-plane upgrades must support a safe rollback path that avoids left-behind stub zones or split-horizon drift. This PR implements an encrypted ENI bootstrapping flow in the Terraform installer, adds a deterministic IP assignment mode for controlplane subnets, introduces a lightweight Helm chart hook to seed a DNS mirror with signed zone metadata, and wires an automated rollback hook that re-scopes stub zone records and removes ephemeral mirror records on failed upgrades. Scope: terraform/modules/network/controlplane/* - new encrypted_eni module and deterministic_ip submodule; helm/controlplane/templates/dns-mirror-hook.yaml - pre-upgrade job to snapshot signed-records and post-failure job to reconcile; installer/upgrade/rollback.sh - idempotent rollback logic and a dry-run verifier; docs/installer/networking.md - operational notes and migration steps; test/terraform/controlplane_integ.tf - added integration fixture for encrypted ENI with mock KMS. Design notes: ENI encryption leverages customer KMS keys passed through the private-installer values and uses an immutable tag pattern (redwood:eni:hash) to detect re-attachment vs replacement during upgrades. Deterministic IP mode exposes a small allocator that maps a CIDR chunk + index -> stable IP and records the mapping in a tfstate-backed lock file so re-applies are stable across operators. DNS mirror hook writes signed RRsets into a namespace-backed ConfigMap and uses TTL pinning to avoid cache stomping; the rollback job reverses TTL pinning and prunes records only when lock metadata indicates the mirror was created by this release. Backwards compatibility: existing installs remain unchanged unless operators enable encrypted_eni or deterministic_ip via the new installer variables. Testing: added unit tests for allocator, a Helm integration run in CI that exercises pre-upgrade snapshot and a staged rollback scenario. Risks & mitigations: key rotation - the module refuses to re-encrypt existing ENIs without an explicit force flag; network policy mismatch - included a preflight verifier that checks security group rules against expected controlplane rule set. Checklist: - [x] Terraform module and unit tests - [x] Helm hook and rollback job - [x] Installer orchestration script - [x] Docs and migration guide - [x] CI integration run Summary of changes, commits and tests below.

title: Emit audit events during Private install/upgrade/rollback/config apply
preview: Context: Private customers reported gaps in audit coverage for operator-driven installer actions (notably upgrades and RBAC-related config applies) and Security needs parity with Hosted/Dedicated for “sensitive actions.” This PR instruments the Private installer to emit audit events for install/upgrade/rollback/config-apply/credential-rotation flows using the audit schema v1 envelope and event_type taxonomy. Summary of changes: (1) Add an audit emitter to the installer runtime that writes v1-shaped events to the local audit spool and forwards to the cluster audit ingestion endpoint when configured. (2) Emit events at the start + completion of sensitive workflows with consistent correlation (admin_action_id) and stable event_id for dedupe. (3) Capture operator identity where available (kubeconfig user / OIDC subject / OS user) without logging secrets. (4) Include deployment context: private_installation_id, cluster_id (if known), namespace, installer_version, and target version for upgrades. Event types added: private.install.start, private.install.complete, private.upgrade.start, private.upgrade.complete, private.rollback.start, private.rollback.complete, private.config.apply, private.credential.rotate. Field behavior: schema_version is set to 1.0; actor.type is “human” when we can resolve a non-empty operator identity and “system” when running unattended; actor.id is set to a stable string (OIDC sub preferred; else kubeconfig user; else local OS username) and actor.display_name is best-effort. source.ip is best-effort (only when invoked via the controller/remote runner, not local CLI). target.type is “private_installation”; target.id is the private_installation_id. changes.summary includes a capped diff summary (max 1KB) for config apply and upgrade plan; raw manifests are never logged. Delivery semantics: best-effort to remote audit sink; guaranteed local spool write unless disk is full. On sink failure, the installer continues and records audit_sink_status=degraded in the event outcome.reason. Tests: add unit tests for event shape validation + redaction, plus an integration test that runs an upgrade dry-run in CI and asserts emitted events match the required fields list. Notes: This is aligned with ENG-4826 and the schema enforcement work in ENG-4821/ENG-4830; exporter normalization (schema_version tagging) remains in audit-log-exporter PRs but this PR ensures producers already emit correctly shaped v1 events. Follow-ups: add operator identity support for air-gapped installs using explicit --operator-id flag (tracked separately), and wire installer audit spool rotation metrics into the Private observability pack.

title: Add upgrade diagnostics bundle + failure hints for stuck migrations
preview:
Context: Support escalations for Private upgrades getting stuck at migrations are taking too long to triage because we’re missing a consistent artifact set and the installer’s failure output doesn’t guide the operator to the right next step. This PR adds a first-class diagnostics bundle and improves upgrade/migrations failure hints so Support/SRE/Platform can move from “stuck” -> “actionable” quickly. Ties to SUP-1845 and ENG-4828.

What’s included:
- New command: `redwood-private-installer diagnostics bundle --reason upgrade --output <path>`
  - Produces a single `.tar.gz` with a predictable directory structure.
  - Explicitly avoids collecting secrets (no K8s Secrets, no env dumps from pods). Basic redaction is applied to common token patterns in logs.
  - Includes a `MANIFEST.txt` with versions and timestamps to make email/Slack handoffs easier.
- Upgrade/migrations aware collection:
  - Installer logs for the attempted run (including last ~5k lines, plus full file if available).
  - Kubernetes resources relevant to the upgrade path (namespace-scoped):
    - `kubectl get pods,deploy,sts,job,cronjob -o wide`
    - `kubectl describe` for the migrations Job (if present) and any CrashLooping pods.
    - `kubectl logs` for migrations job pods (current + previous when supported).
    - Recent namespace events (last 2h) and warnings.
  - Helm status/history for the control plane release when deployed via Helm (non-fatal if not present).
  - Node pressure summary (disk/mem) from `kubectl describe node` (capped to the node(s) hosting control plane pods; best-effort).
- Clearer failure hints:
  - If the upgrade appears stuck at migrations (job running > threshold or repeated restarts), print a “What to check next” section with the exact commands to run and what output to attach.
  - Detect common classes of failures and emit targeted hints:
    - Image pull errors -> check registry reachability / pull secret configuration.
    - RBAC forbidden errors -> verify the installer service account cluster role binding.
    - Postgres connectivity / auth failures -> verify DB endpoint reachability and credentials rotation timing.
    - Schema lock / long-running migration -> provide guidance on collecting DB lock diagnostics (without exposing credentials) and when to escalate.
  - All hints include a “When to escalate” line that points Support to include the diagnostics bundle and reference SUP key(s).

Why this design:
- Support needs a single artifact they can request that doesn’t require live debugging on a customer cluster.
- The bundle format is stable so macros and KB articles can be copy/paste-ready.
- We intentionally do not attempt to auto-remediate. We only collect and guide to avoid surprising changes in regulated environments.

How to test:
- `make test` (unit tests for bundling/redaction + hint classification)
- Manual:
  - Run `diagnostics bundle` on a healthy cluster (should succeed and include expected files).
  - Simulate a failing migrations job (bad DB host) and confirm installer emits hint + bundle includes job logs/events.

Follow-ups (out of scope for this PR):
- Add a Support-facing doc page that mirrors the required artifacts + “stuck migrations” checklist (handled in ENG-4828 rollup).
- Add optional `--since` for events/logs window control (defaults are chosen to keep bundles reasonably sized).

title: airgap: add apt/yum repo metadata + slim runtime bundles for offline installs
preview: Motivation: customers running on-prem/private installs often hit two pain points: (1) large monolithic tarballs that waste bandwidth and storage when only runtime artifacts are needed; (2) lack of standard apt/yum metadata in bundles makes tooling brittle for distributions that expect repo indices. This PR introduces a new bundle layout and tooling to reduce airgap bundle size, support OS package repositories out-of-the-box, and improve reproducible installs. Summary of changes: add airgap repo metadata generator that creates Packages.gz and repodata/repomd.xml for deb/rpm artifacts; add lightweight 'slim' runtime images for init, health-probe and migrator containers (alpine-based with static-linked helpers) and a build pipeline that outputs both full and slim images into the bundle; introduce an 'airgap-manifest.json' (minimal, stable schema v1) that lists package names, shasums, signed-gpg key IDs, and repository indexes for apt/yum + image index for containerd/CRI; update the bundle extractor to optionally populate a local apt/yum HTTP server (repo-proxy) used during install so machines can apt-get/yum install from the local repository without internet access; add helper script to import and rotate GPG keys used for repository verification; update helm chart packaging step to produce a helm-index.yaml inside the bundle and add a small init hook that can serve the chart archive to helm client during install; add integration tests that spawn a temporary HTTP server to validate apt/yum indices and verify package installation in a minimal Ubuntu 20.04 and Rocky Linux 8 chroot; docs updated with step-by-step airgap preparation, size expectations and migration notes. Checklist: - [x] generator for apt/yum metadata - [x] slim runtime images and packaging pipeline - [x] bundle manifest and extractor changes - [x] local repo-proxy implementation and tests - [x] GPG import/rotation helpers - [x] docs and upgrade notes Backwards compatibility: bundle extractor maintains support for the old flat tar layout; new manifest is optional and extractor will fall back if not present. Upgrade strategy: bundles produced by the new pipeline will be backward-compatible to older installers; however, to fully leverage slim images and repo indices, customers should upgrade the installer to >= the release that includes this PR. Related: hits SUP-3124 for on-prem support requests.

title: Private installer: add safe optimization config rollback command (pin + verify)
preview:
Context
Private deployments now support versioned Optimization Configs with safe apply + one-click rollback in Console. Private operators requested an equivalent workflow when Console access is unavailable (break-glass) or when rolling back during cluster/network issues.

This PR adds an operator-friendly rollback command to the `redwood-private-installer` CLI. The workflow is designed to be safe by default (explicit scope + confirmation), idempotent (retries won’t wedge state), and observable (structured logs + correlation IDs).

What’s in this PR
- New CLI command: `rwpi optimize-config rollback`
  - Rolls back the active Optimization Config version for a given scope by calling the Private control plane rollback API.
  - Supports explicit targeting:
    - `--tenant <tenant_slug>` (required)
    - `--model <model_id>` (optional; defaults to tenant-wide if omitted)
    - `--region <region>` (optional; defaults to installation region if omitted)
    - `--to-version <version_id>` (required)
  - Safety guardrails:
    - `--dry-run` prints the computed blast radius and the current/target versions without executing.
    - `--require-active-version` (default true) prevents rollback if the installer cannot confirm the currently active version.
    - Interactive confirmation with “type-to-confirm” using a deterministic confirmation string: `ROLLBACK <tenant>/<model-or-*>/<region-or-*> to <version>`.
    - Optional `--force` to bypass confirmation for automation (still logs a warning).
  - Emits a generated `change_request_id` (UUID) included in installer logs and passed through as an HTTP header for control plane/audit correlation.

- Preflight + verification
  - Preflight checks:
    - Installer can reach the control plane endpoint and is authenticated.
    - Target version exists and is compatible with the requested scope.
    - RBAC check: operator token must have the rollback permission.
  - Post-action verification:
    - Polls the control plane “active version” endpoint until it reports the target version or timeout.
    - Prints clear next steps when verification fails (includes curl commands and where to look in control plane logs).

- Installer state tracking (for safer operator workflows)
  - Records the last observed active version per scope in the installer state file to make it easier to revert during repeated incidents.
  - Does not attempt to be the source of truth; it is only used to improve operator UX (the control plane remains authoritative).

- Documentation
  - Adds `docs/operator/optimization-config-rollback.md`:
    - Example rollbacks
    - What “rolled back” means in Private (control plane pointer update + rollout controller applies to workers)
    - Common failure modes (RBAC denied, scope mismatch, verification timeout)

Example
- Dry run:
  `rwpi optimize-config rollback --tenant acme --model llama-3-70b --region us-east-1 --to-version ocv_01J0Z9... --dry-run`
- Execute:
  `rwpi optimize-config rollback --tenant acme --model llama-3-70b --region us-east-1 --to-version ocv_01J0Z9...`

Changed areas (high level)
- cmd/rwpi: command registration + flags
- internal/controlplane: API client additions:
  - GET active optimization config version (for verification)
  - POST rollback (with `change_request_id` propagation)
- internal/operator: confirmation + blast-radius formatting helpers
- docs: operator guide
- tests: unit tests + lightweight kind smoke test

Notes / design choices
- This PR intentionally does not implement fully automatic rollback loops. The command requires explicit target version and an operator confirmation by default.
- Verification polling defaults to 90s with exponential backoff; can be overridden via `--verify-timeout`.
- If the control plane rollback returns success but verification can’t confirm, the command exits non-zero to encourage explicit operator follow-up.

Rollout plan
- Internal use first (Redwood SRE + Private deployments on-call) during gamedays.
- Publish to Private operator docs after GA feature flag is enabled in Private control plane.

Follow-ups
- Add optional integration to annotate the customer’s Prometheus (if present) with rollback events.
- Support reading candidate rollback targets from the control plane version history to reduce manual copy/paste of version IDs.

Refs
- Linear: ENG-4829 (Private deployments sync + rollback), ENG-4825 (rollback endpoint)
- Related (monorepo): runtime rollback race fix in redwood/pr-20111

title: deterministic-gpu-nodepool-stencil-tfmodule-checkpoint
preview: Context & motivation: Private deployments require predictable nodepool identities and safer rollback semantics when operators apply changes across air-gapped or peered VPCs. Existing modules create GPU nodepools with provider-generated names and rely on global tfstate only; this makes cross-account reconciliation and on-prem rollback brittle. This PR introduces a new terraform module (modules/gpu-nodepool-stencil) that provides deterministic naming, optional lease-backed ephemeral IAM bindings, and a lightweight tfstate checkpointing mechanism anchored to network tags. Goals: 1) deterministic, stable nodepool identifiers to allow safe rebinds and tenant moves; 2) lease-based IAM sessions so nodepools get least-privilege, short-lived credentials for cluster bootstrap; 3) checkpointed state objects (S3/GCS) tied to VPC/subnet tags to support orchestrated rollbacks and faster drift detection. What changed: - New module: terraform/modules/gpu-nodepool-stencil/ implementing deterministic naming strategy, nodepool fencing IAM templates, and optional network-anchor checkpointing hook. - New outputs and inputs to make module pluggable into existing private-installer stacks (vpc_id, subnet_tags, tenant_id, naming_seed). - Add tfstate-checkpoint script (scripts/tfstate_checkpoint.sh) used as a post-apply hook to write a minimal JSON checkpoint object to configured object store with network tag metadata. - Add migrator: scripts/tfstate_migrator_detname.go to compute deterministic names and optionally migrate state entries for existing installs (dry-run mode by default). - Add e2e-local test harness under test/integration/gpu-nodepool-stencil/ that runs a plan/apply against a local GCS-compatible backend (minio) to validate checkpoint creation and IAM binding lifecycle. - Documentation: docs/private-deploy/gpu-nodepool-stencil.md with examples and migration steps. - CI: terraform fmt/lint, unit tests for naming math, and an integration smoke test. Backwards compatibility & migration: The migrator is intentionally opt-in. The module has a toggle 'adopt_existing_names' and 'migrator_mode' with 'dry-run' by default; operators must run the migrator and confirm checkpoints before flipping adoption flags. Checklist: - [x] Terraform module implemented with full variable validation - [x] Post-apply checkpoint hook implemented and tested against minio - [x] Migrator included and documented - [x] Unit tests for naming algorithm - [x] Integration smoke test added - [x] Docs added and linked in private-installer README Testing & validation: Ran CI pipeline (format/lint/plan) — initially one formatting error, fixed in commit 3. Integration harness executed end-to-end on local minio and created checkpoint objects with expected metadata. Security & IAM: The module creates IAM role templates only; actual role assumption is gated by a 'lease' provider that issues ephemeral tokens (TTL configurable). The default behavior is conservative (no IAM creation unless 'enable_ephemeral_iam' is true). Migration notes: Run scripts/tfstate_migrator_detname.go --dry-run to preview changes, then create checkpoints for current state, then flip 'adopt_existing_names'. Related work: ENG-6342 (deterministic node ids), ENG-6401 (rollback checkpoints).

title: Add break-glass flag for bundle verification bypass + explicit audit warning
preview:
Context: We now fail-closed on offline bundle signature/manifest verification during preflight. For a small set of air-gapped environments, we still need an emergency, explicitly-audited path to proceed when verification cannot be performed (e.g., corrupted trust store update package, temporary time source issues, or operational incident). This PR introduces a break-glass override flag that is policy-gated and leaves strong evidence (console warnings + local audit markers) when used.

What this PR does: Adds a break-glass path for preflight/install that allows skipping bundle signature verification only when an explicit local policy allows it. The override is intentionally noisy and requires operators to provide a reason + approval reference, and we emit audit events for both attempted and successful usage.

CLI / UX changes: - New flag: --break-glass-skip-bundle-verification (alias: --break-glass) - Required when break-glass is used: --break-glass-reason <string> and --break-glass-approval <string> (e.g., Jira SUP-31977 / internal incident ID / customer change ticket) - Optional: --break-glass-operator <string> (defaults to current OS user if resolvable) - When triggered, installer prints a multi-line WARNING banner before proceeding, including a summary of what checks are being bypassed, and paths to logs/support bundle. - Break-glass is blocked by default unless policy explicitly allows it.

Policy gating: Break-glass requires one of the following (in priority order): 1) /etc/redwood/installer/policy.yaml contains allow_break_glass: true 2) --policy-file <path> pointing to a policy yaml with allow_break_glass: true If neither is present/enabled, the installer fails with a clear error and does not proceed.

Audit markers / telemetry: - Emits local audit event: private_installer.bundle_verification.override_attempted (always when flags are supplied) - Emits local audit event: private_installer.bundle_verification.override_used (only if policy allows and we proceed) - Event includes: bundle_build_id (if readable), bundle_manifest_digest (if present), bundle_path, installer_version, reason, approval, operator, host_id (hashed), timestamp, and a boolean indicating whether verification was skipped vs succeeded. - Also writes an explicit marker into the install state record (state.json) so subsequent support bundles can see that the current install was performed under break-glass.

Safety rails: - Break-glass only affects the bundle signature verification step (manifest signature + trust store). Hash verification of the manifest file itself is not possible without a trusted signature, so we do not attempt partial verification that could be misconstrued as trustworthy. - We do NOT change any behavior around network access: in air-gapped mode, installer still will not attempt to fetch missing artifacts. - Break-glass is not available for the inspect command unless the same policy gate is satisfied, to reduce casual misuse.

Testing: - Unit tests for flag parsing, required reason/approval enforcement, and policy gate behavior. - Integration tests for: (a) break-glass blocked by default, (b) break-glass allowed with policy file, (c) audit event emitted and contains required fields, (d) install state marker set when used.

Files/areas changed (high level): - cmd/private-installer/main.go: wiring new flags and help text - internal/preflight/verify_bundle.go: add override path + structured errors - internal/policy/policy.go: read allow_break_glass from policy yaml (existing parser extended) - internal/audit/events.go + internal/audit/emitter.go: new event types + severity WARN mapping - internal/state/state.go: persist break-glass marker + reason/approval reference - docs/cli.md: document flags, example usage, and support expectations

Example output (trimmed): WARNING: BREAK-GLASS MODE ENABLED. Bundle signature verification has been SKIPPED. This increases supply-chain risk and must only be used with explicit approval. Reason: "Customer time source unavailable (air-gapped)" Approval: "SUP-31977" Audit event written to: /var/log/redwood-installer/audit.log

Rollout notes: - This is intended for emergency use only; normal installs should never use break-glass. - Security/GRC requested explicit evidence on usage; audit markers are designed to be SIEM-exportable in later work.

Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Wire the new audit event into the audit-log-exporter schema (tracked separately) - Add a support-bundle section that highlights break-glass usage prominently (partially addressed via state marker; formatting improvements later)

Checklist: - [x] Requires policy gate (default deny) - [x] Requires reason + approval reference - [x] Emits audit events for attempted/used - [x] Leaves local state marker - [x] Tests cover deny/allow paths - [x] Docs updated

Risk assessment: This change intentionally introduces a bypass, but it is tightly gated, noisy, and auditable. The default posture remains fail-closed. The primary risk is operator misuse; mitigations are (1) policy requirement, (2) required approval reference, (3) persistent audit evidence.

Refs: ENG-4824 (break-glass override design and policy), ENG-4823 (installer verification fail-closed), SUP-31977 (support process guidance request).

title: Docs: update air-gapped installation flow for signed offline bundles
preview:
Summary: Updates the in-repo installer documentation to reflect the new signed offline bundle workflow used for air-gapped installs. This PR is intentionally focused on README/docs content and examples (no behavior changes), and aligns terminology with recent installer work: bundle manifest, detached signature, trust store, preflight verification, downgrade prevention, and break-glass override logging.

Why: Pilot customers and Support/SEs were following the older “copy bundle + run install” flow and missing the required verification step. In addition, the new tooling (bundle inspect/verify) and the fail-closed behavior in preflight needs to be documented with the exact command lines and expected outputs so air-gapped operators can self-diagnose issues like hash mismatch, unknown key, and time skew.

What changed (docs only): 1) Rewrote the Air-gapped Installation section to make verification an explicit required step before install. 2) Added a “Bundle layout” explanation with examples (manifest + manifest.sig alongside artifacts). 3) Added a troubleshooting table mapping common error codes/messages to remediation steps and where logs are written. 4) Documented how to supply/refresh the trust store in air-gapped environments (including key rotation expectations). 5) Documented break-glass override flag usage as a last resort, including audit implications and required follow-up.

Key examples added: - Verify bundle signature and manifest before install: `rwi bundle inspect --bundle ./redwood-private-offline-bundle-2026.01.0.tgz --verify` - Run preflight in air-gapped mode (fails closed on verification): `rwi install preflight --air-gapped --bundle ./redwood-private-offline-bundle-2026.01.0.tgz` - Install from a verified bundle: `rwi install --air-gapped --bundle ./redwood-private-offline-bundle-2026.01.0.tgz` - Explicitly point to a trust store file (for rotation updates): `rwi install preflight --air-gapped --bundle ./bundle.tgz --trust-store ./trust/redwood-trust.json` - Break-glass (policy gated): `rwi install --air-gapped --bundle ./bundle.tgz --break-glass-allow-unsigned` (docs emphasize this emits warnings/audit markers and should only be used with approval)

Notes: - These docs assume the signed bundle layout introduced by recent work (detached signature file) and the preflight verification behavior from ENG-4823. - Time skew guidance is included (air-gapped environments frequently lack NTP); docs include a short note on diagnosing “not yet valid” failures and collecting the system clock/time source details for Support.

Testing: - `make lint` - `make test` - `make docs-check` (markdown link checker) - Local verification of rendered README code blocks + copy/paste command sanity check against current installer CLI help output.

Follow-ups: - The public docs site update is tracked separately in redwood-docs; this PR is specifically for in-repo installer docs that ship with the on-prem package.

title: local-registry-fast-sync-and-rollback-snapshots
preview: Motivation: Private on-prem installs frequently hit long bootstrap windows when seeding large OCI registries over slow links. This PR introduces a delta-aware fast-sync engine plus snapshot-based rollback anchors so customers can perform atomic upgrades and quickly revert to a known registry state in air-gapped or VPC environments. What changed: 1) new Terraform module redwood.local_registry that orchestrates an initial seeding plan and creates a signed snapshot manifest, 2) a sync agent (installer/bin/registry-sync) that performs checksum-driven delta pulls, resumes interrupted transfers, and applies layer-level parallelism tuned for low-latency paper networks, 3) snapshot manifest format and a lightweight anchor scheme (snap-<epoch>-<seq>) stored in the registry under a signed tag, 4) Helm chart additions: registry-mirror-init sidecar, offline-values configmap templating and a preflight hook to validate snapshot compatibility, 5) upgrade/rollback tools: installer/bin/rollback-to-snapshot that verifies chain integrity before promoting a snapshot atomically, 6) docs and runbooks under docs/installer/reg-sync.md and runbooks/rollback-playbook.md, 7) tests: e2e against a local OCI mirror and unit tests for manifest signing/verify. Rollback approach: snapshots are created before any upgrade step, indexed by monotonically increasing sequence and a signed digest. Rollback validates that the registry contains all referenced layers and performs an atomic tag swap using a temporary tag promotion flow to avoid partial reads. Backwards compatibility: the manifest schema v1 is backward compatible with our existing sealed bundle indexer; the Terraform module offers both a 'seed' and 'resume' mode. Testing and validation: ran offline e2e in a simulated 10Mbps link with large model blobs; median bootstrap time reduced 4.2x versus baseline, resume tests passed on network interruption, and rollback exercise completed within expected latency budget. Security considerations: manifests are signed with customer KMS keys when available; sidecar runs as non-root. Migration notes: existing install workflows that rely on the previous registry seeding script can opt-in to 'fast_sync=true' in the installer config. Checklist: - [x] Terraform module + docs - [x] sync agent binary + unit tests - [x] Helm chart hooks and templates - [x] rollback tool + runbook - [x] e2e validation and CI integration - [x] docs and example values How to validate locally: follow docs/installer/local-fast-sync.md to bring up a miniregistry, run terraform apply in examples/registry-seed, then run installer/bin/registry-sync --plan examples/plan.json. Related: follows discussions in INFRA-234 and reuses part of the delta-indexing code introduced in ENG-42099.

title: Prevent offline bundle downgrade using signed manifest version + local install state
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds downgrade protection for Redwood Private offline bundles by enforcing monotonic install versions based on signed manifest metadata and a local install state record. After signature verification succeeds, the installer compares the bundle's signed version against the last successfully installed version recorded on disk; if the incoming bundle is older, preflight fails closed with an actionable error.

## Why
Air-gapped customers frequently move bundles around via removable media. Without downgrade protection, an attacker (or an operational mistake) can replay an older-but-valid signed bundle to reintroduce known vulnerabilities or regress critical fixes. Because our threat model includes replay/downgrade as a realistic risk in offline workflows, we need the installer to defend against accidental or malicious rollbacks by default.

## Design
- New signed manifest fields consumed by the installer:
  - `bundleVersion` (string): semantic-ish version used for ordering (e.g., `2026.01.0`, `2026.01.1-rc.2`). This field is already covered by the manifest signature.
  - `buildId` (string): opaque build identifier for support correlation (not used for ordering, but recorded).
- New local install state record:
  - Path: `/var/lib/redwood-private-installer/install-state.json` (Linux default). The path is configurable via `--state-dir` for non-standard environments.
  - Contents recorded after successful install only: `lastInstalledVersion`, `lastInstalledBuildId`, `lastVerifiedManifestSha256`, `lastVerifiedKeyId`, `installedAt`.
  - File permissions: created with `0600` where possible; directory with `0700`.
- Enforcement:
  - Preflight flow becomes:
    1) Load + verify manifest signature (existing behavior from PR-24844).
    2) Parse `bundleVersion` from signed manifest.
    3) Load install state (if present).
    4) If `bundleVersion` < `lastInstalledVersion`, fail preflight with `RPI-VER-009` (DowngradeBlocked).
  - If no install state exists (fresh install), no downgrade check is applied.

## Version ordering
We need stable ordering that behaves sensibly across our release trains:
- Supports `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` plus optional prerelease tags (e.g., `-rc.1`).
- Treats prerelease as lower than the corresponding stable release: `2026.01.0-rc.2` < `2026.01.0`.
- If parsing fails for either side, we fail closed with `RPI-VER-010` (UnparseableVersion) and instruct the operator to use a newer bundle/installer or contact Support.

## Operator experience
Example error:
`ERROR RPI-VER-009: Downgrade blocked. Bundle version 2026.01.0 is older than last installed version 2026.01.2 on this host. Downgrades are disabled by default to prevent replay of older signed bundles. If you are attempting an approved rollback, contact Redwood Support for the documented break-glass procedure.`

Notes:
- This PR does NOT add the break-glass override flag; that is tracked separately to ensure we get security policy + audit semantics right. For now, rollback requires following the documented support procedure (manual remediation) and is intentionally not self-serve.

## Test plan
- Unit tests for version parsing and ordering (stable vs prerelease, invalid strings, edge cases).
- Installer integration test: install bundle A (writes state), attempt bundle older than A (blocked), attempt newer bundle (allowed), ensure state updates only after successful install.
- Manual: verified on an offline VM with existing signed bundle artifacts; verified error messages are surfaced in both `preflight` and full `install` path.

## Security notes
- The install-state file is not a security root of trust; it is an enforcement mechanism to prevent accidental/operational downgrade. An attacker with root access could tamper with it; that threat is out of scope for offline bundle verification controls and is covered by host hardening expectations.
- The ordering decision is based on signed manifest metadata, so tampering with the bundle version requires invalidating the signature.

## Follow-ups
- Add audited break-glass override flag (ENG-4824) with explicit local audit events.
- Document rollback procedure in air-gapped guide + runbooks.
- Consider pinning minimum allowed version per cluster in a cluster-scoped state record (future).

Checklist
- [x] Added tests
- [x] Updated error taxonomy (RPI-VER-009/010)
- [x] Verified state file permissions
- [x] Validated behavior on fresh install vs upgrade

Refs
- Linear: ENG-4827 (tamper/replay/downgrade), ENG-4823 (verification fail-closed)
- Threat model: Offline bundle signing (replay/downgrade mitigation)

Because our threat model includes replay/downgrade as a realistic risk in offline workflows, we need the installer to defend against accidental or malicious rollbacks by default.

## Design
- New signed manifest fields consumed by the installer:
  - `bundleVersion` (string): semantic-ish version used for ordering (e.g., `2026.01.0`, `2026.01.1-rc.2`). This field is already covered by the manifest signature.
  - `buildId` (string): opaque build identifier for support correlation (not used for ordering, but recorded).
- New local install state record:
  - Path: `/var/lib/redwood-private-installer/install-state.json` (Linux default). The path is configurable via `--state-dir` for non-standard environments.
  - Contents recorded after successful install only: `lastInstalledVersion`, `lastInstalledBuildId`, `lastVerifiedManifestSha256`, `lastVerifiedKeyId`, `installedAt`.
  - File permissions: created with `0600` where possible; directory with `0700`.
- Enforcement:
  - Preflight flow becomes:
    1) Load + verify manifest signature (existing behavior from PR-24844).
    2) Parse `bundleVersion` from signed manifest.
    3) Load install state (if present).
    4) If `bundleVersion` < `lastInstalledVersion`, fail preflight with `RPI-VER-009` (DowngradeBlocked).
  - If no install state exists (fresh install), no downgrade check is applied.

## Version ordering
We need stable ordering that behaves sensibly across our release trains:
- Supports `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` plus optional prerelease tags (e.g., `-rc.1`).
- Treats prerelease as lower than the corresponding stable release: `2026.01.0-rc.2` < `2026.01.0`.
- If parsing fails for either side, we fail closed with `RPI-VER-010` (UnparseableVersion) and instruct the operator to use a newer bundle/installer or contact Support.

## Operator experience
Example error:
`ERROR RPI-VER-009: Downgrade blocked. Bundle version 2026.01.0 is older than last installed version 2026.01.2 on this host. Downgrades are disabled by default to prevent replay of older signed bundles. If you are attempting an approved rollback, contact Redwood Support for the documented break-glass procedure.`

Notes:
- This PR does NOT add the break-glass override flag; that is tracked separately to ensure we get security policy + audit semantics right. For now, rollback requires following the documented support procedure (manual remediation) and is intentionally not self-serve.

## Test plan
- Unit tests for version parsing and ordering (stable vs prerelease, invalid strings, edge cases).
- Installer integration test: install bundle A (writes state), attempt bundle older than A (blocked), attempt newer bundle (allowed), ensure state updates only after successful install.
- Manual: verified on an offline VM with existing signed bundle artifacts; verified error messages are surfaced in both `preflight` and full `install` path.

## Security notes
- The install-state file is not a security root of trust; it is an enforcement mechanism to prevent accidental/operational downgrade. An attacker with root access could tamper with it; that threat is out of scope for offline bundle verification controls and is covered by host hardening expectations.
- The ordering decision is based on signed manifest metadata, so tampering with the bundle version requires invalidating the signature.

## Follow-ups
- Add audited break-glass override flag (ENG-4824) with explicit local audit events.
- Document rollback procedure in air-gapped guide + runbooks.
- Consider pinning minimum allowed version per cluster in a cluster-scoped state record (future).

Checklist
- [x] Added tests
- [x] Updated error taxonomy (RPI-VER-009/010)
- [x] Verified state file permissions
- [x] Validated behavior on fresh install vs upgrade

Refs
- Linear: ENG-4827 (tamper/replay/downgrade), ENG-4823 (verification fail-closed)
- Threat model: Offline bundle signing (replay/downgrade mitigation)

title: Add SBOM pointer + sha256 to offline bundle manifest and verify offline
preview: Context: Air-gapped customers are asking for a verifiable SBOM as part of the offline bundle so they can do internal intake (security scanning / procurement) without relying on external links. We already sign the manifest and verify artifact hashes; this PR extends the manifest to reference the SBOM artifact(s) and verifies the SBOM file hash offline during preflight. Summary of changes: (1) Manifest schema: adds an optional `sbom` entry with fields for `uri` (relative path in bundle or file:// URI), `sha256` (hex), and `format` (e.g., spdx-json, cyclonedx-json). The SBOM entry is included in the signed manifest payload, so tampering is detected via signature verification and also via explicit hash verification. (2) Bundle layout convention: recommends placing SBOMs under `sbom/` in the bundle root (e.g., `sbom/redwood-private-<version>.spdx.json`). The manifest stores the relative path (preferred) or file:// URI for non-standard layouts. We explicitly do NOT support fetching SBOMs over the network during install; if the manifest uses http(s) and `--air-gapped` is enabled, preflight fails with an actionable error. (3) Installer preflight: adds a verification step `verifySbom()` executed after manifest signature verification and before chart/image checks. Behavior: if `sbom` is present, the installer resolves the SBOM file path, computes sha256, compares to manifest, and fails closed on mismatch; if the SBOM file is missing, it fails in air-gapped mode and warns (non-fatal) in connected mode (to preserve forward-compat while we migrate internal bundle builders). If `sbom` is not present, preflight proceeds with a single warning line noting SBOM verification was skipped. (4) Tests: adds unit tests for schema parse + canonicalization (sbom fields included), and preflight verification tests covering: missing sbom file, mismatched hash, and a valid SBOM hash. Adds a regression test to ensure we never attempt network access in air-gapped mode even if a URI is mistakenly set to https://. (5) UX: introduces specific error codes/messages used by Support: `BUNDLE_SBOM_NOT_FOUND`, `BUNDLE_SBOM_HASH_MISMATCH`, `BUNDLE_SBOM_URI_UNSUPPORTED_OFFLINE`. Includes remediation tips (verify transfer integrity, re-extract bundle, confirm you have the correct SBOM file that matches the bundle build id). Implementation notes: The manifest remains canonicalized via our existing stable JSON encoder; the new `sbom` fields are treated as normal keys and appear deterministically. We intentionally kept this additive (no schema version bump) to avoid bricking older bundles; older installer versions will ignore the new fields, but this installer will verify them when present. Follow-ups (out of scope here): (a) make SBOM presence required for GA bundles (gated in release tooling), (b) allow multiple SBOMs (per-component) if compliance asks; current schema supports a single top-level SBOM pointer which is sufficient for pilot customers. Security considerations: This reduces supply-chain risk for offline deployments by ensuring the SBOM itself is integrity-protected and tied to the exact bundle contents. It also prevents a class of “swap SBOM during transfer” issues that came up during customer security reviews. Related: ENG-4821 (manifest schema/canonicalization), ENG-4823 (installer verification), ENG-4829 (audit events for verification outcomes; this PR does not add new audit events beyond existing preflight result logging).

title: Wire signed offline bundle metadata into generated support bundle
preview: Context: With signed offline bundles, Support/SEs frequently need to answer "did verification run" and "what exactly was verified" from a support bundle alone (esp. in air-gapped environments where customers share a tarball via offline transport). Today the support bundle includes installer logs and config, but it does not summarize the offline bundle’s manifest/signature state, leading to longer back-and-forth (SUP-31844) and mis-triage when transport corrupts artifacts (SUP-31892). This PR adds a structured summary file and a small amount of diagnostic output so that a support bundle can stand alone for first-pass triage without exposing sensitive customer data. What this changes: (1) Adds a new support bundle attachment: support/bundle-verification.json containing the last-known offline bundle verification result, plus key metadata pulled from the signed manifest and verifier. (2) Includes the raw verification error code + remediation hint (not the full stack trace) to align with the installer’s verification error taxonomy. (3) Captures bundle identity fields needed to correlate to release artifacts: bundle_build_id, bundle_version, manifest_digest_sha256, signature_digest_sha256, signer_key_id, trust_store_version, and installer_version. (4) Adds a "verification_context" block that records whether verification ran during preflight, whether break-glass override was used, and any detected time-skew handling path (without recording the host clock). Safety/redaction: no file paths outside the bundle root are recorded; no customer tokens/URLs are included; the trust store content is not dumped, only the configured trust store version/id and the selected key id. Implementation notes: the support bundle generator now consumes the same in-memory VerificationReport object returned by preflight (or loads the persisted report if present) and writes a stable JSON file using canonical key ordering for diffs. If verification has never been run, the support bundle records status=unknown and includes next steps. Testing: unit tests cover "success", "hash mismatch", "unknown key", "not yet valid" and "break-glass" cases; integration test verifies support bundle output is produced in air-gapped mode. Release note: none (internal supportability improvement), but docs/runbook owners have been notified to reference the new file in triage steps.

title: Improve offline bundle verification error messages + remediation hints
preview:
Context: With signed offline bundles, verification failures are expected to be one of the top support drivers during pilot. Today, failures often surface as generic crypto/library errors with no guidance on what to do next, and logs are not consistently referenced in the CLI output. This PR keeps fail-closed behavior unchanged, but makes the UX/support story significantly better.

What this changes: - Introduces a structured error taxonomy for bundle verification with stable error codes (RV-VERIFY-0xxx). - Maps common failure modes to a short 'what happened' + 'what to do next' remediation block, including where to find logs. - Adds a single-line summary suitable for copy/paste into tickets, plus a verbose output behind --verbose. - Normalizes log locations across Linux packages and containerized installer runs, and prints the resolved log path in the error footer. - Adds a 'transport integrity' remediation hint for hash mismatches seen in SUP-31892 (zip/unzip tooling, line-ending conversions, partial copies). - Adds time-skew specific messaging (not-yet-valid / expired) including suggested checks for air-gapped time sources.

Non-goals: - No change to verification policy (still blocks install on invalid/untrusted signatures). - No change to signature format / manifest schema (handled in other PRs). - No break-glass flag in this PR (tracked separately).

Testing: - Added unit tests for error mapping and formatting. - Updated integration tests to assert error codes + presence of remediation hints. - Manually validated output on Ubuntu 22.04 and RHEL 8 with tampered manifest + unknown key cases.

Docs: This PR updates the in-repo installer help text for the new error codes and log path footer. The full customer-facing docs update is tracked in ENG-4850.

Related PRs: - pr-24844-verify-bundle-signature-during-preflight - pr-24859-add-trust-store-and-key-rollover-support

title: Add integration tests for tampered offline bundles (fail closed)
preview: Context: As part of offline bundle signing + verification, we need high-signal integration tests that simulate real customer failure modes (tampering during transfer, swapped artifacts, partial copies) and ensure the installer preflight fails closed with actionable errors. This PR adds an integration test suite that generates/uses a minimal signed test bundle fixture and then applies targeted corruption steps: (1) modified manifest contents (JSON canonicalization changes + byte edits), (2) swapped artifact file while leaving manifest unchanged, (3) truncated bundle archive (simulating partial rsync/usb copy), and (4) missing signature file for detached layout. Each scenario asserts that `installer preflight` exits non-zero, emits the expected error code, and does not proceed to any install step. The suite also verifies that the error taxonomy remains stable (Support can map failures) and that logs include the verification reason without dumping sensitive bundle contents. Notes: The tests intentionally avoid any network usage and use a temporary isolated work dir to ensure they run in CI with outbound disabled. Related: ENG-4827, ENG-4823. Support context: SUP-31892 (hash mismatch after transfer) informed the truncated/corrupted archive coverage. Checklist: - [x] Add tamper fixtures + helpers - [x] Add integration coverage for manifest edit / artifact swap / archive truncation - [x] Assert fail-closed behavior (no install) - [x] Validate error codes + log redaction - [x] Run tests under `NO_NETWORK=1` in CI - [x] Document how to re-generate fixtures locally

title: Refactor bundle download-disabled paths (air-gapped mode never fetches missing artifacts)
preview:
Context: We’ve had multiple pilot/poc environments where outbound egress is hard-disabled (or DNS is blackholed), but the installer still *attempts* to fetch “helpful” missing artifacts (chart tarballs, index.yaml, image manifests) when an offline bundle is incomplete or the user points at the wrong bundle path. In air-gapped installs this produces long timeouts and confusing errors, and more importantly violates the expectation that air-gapped mode never reaches out to the network.

This PR hardens the download-disabled paths by (1) centralizing network policy enforcement and (2) making missing-offline-artifact handling fail-fast with actionable remediation.

What changed: - Introduced a single NetworkPolicy gate (AllowOutbound|DenyOutbound) wired into all downloader call sites. If DenyOutbound is active, any attempt to invoke a downloader returns a typed error immediately (no retries, no DNS resolution, no proxy evaluation). - Refactored bundle resolution so that “offline bundle present?” and “artifact present in bundle?” checks happen *before* we construct remote URLs. - Removed legacy fallback behavior that would try to pull Helm chart tarballs from the release CDN when the offline bundle chart directory was missing. - Updated preflight to explicitly enumerate required artifacts for the chosen install mode and fail closed when any are missing. Error includes: missing path(s), expected bundle layout hint, and a reminder to run `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect` (added in pr-24888) for a complete report.

Behavioral changes (user-visible): - `--air-gapped` (and config `network.outbound=disabled`) now guarantees zero outbound requests from the installer process. If the bundle is incomplete, the install stops during preflight with a deterministic error. - Timeout-style failures (waiting on dead DNS / blocked egress) are replaced with immediate, actionable failures.

Implementation notes: - Added `internal/netpolicy` with a lightweight interface (`AssertOutboundAllowed(ctx, op)`) that is called in: chart loader, image list resolver, optional SBOM pointer fetcher, and telemetry exporter init. - Error taxonomy: new error code `RPIE-NET-001` for outbound attempts while outbound is disabled, and `RPIE-BUNDLE-014` for missing required offline artifact. Both are surfaced in console output and logs.

Testing: - Added unit tests for netpolicy gating (ensures no downloader stubs are invoked). - Added integration test covering the regression: air-gapped install with missing `charts/` directory must fail fast and must not call the HTTP transport (uses a test HTTP round-tripper that panics if invoked). - Reproduced SUP-31892 scenario locally using a zip/unzip workflow that drops symlinks; preflight now reports the specific missing paths.

Rollout/risk: - Change is scoped to air-gapped/outbound-disabled modes; default (online) behavior still supports downloading optional artifacts where configured. - This is a compatibility tightening: installs that previously “sort of worked” by downloading missing charts will now fail unless the bundle is complete. This is desired for Private/air-gapped and aligns with the signed bundle work.

Docs follow-up: Separate PR will update the in-repo README to mention the stricter behavior and point users to `bundle inspect` output for support cases.

References: ENG-4823 (installer preflight verification and fail-closed), ENG-4827 (integration tests tamper/replay/downgrade), SUP-31892 (hash mismatch after transfer / incomplete bundle symptoms).
