title: Emit local audit/telemetry events for offline bundle verification outcomes (with redaction)
preview:
Summary: This PR emits local audit/telemetry events during offline bundle signature+manifest verification. Events cover both success and failure paths with a stable schema and an explicit redaction policy suitable for air-gapped/regulated environments. The installer continues to make no outbound network calls; events are written locally and can later be exported via the existing audit-log-exporter integration (when present) or collected via support bundles.

Motivation: Support and customer security teams asked for an auditable record of verification outcomes to answer: (1) did the installer verify the bundle? (2) why did it fail? (3) which trust root was used? and (4) did anyone use override/break-glass. Prior behavior only printed CLI output and wrote unstructured logs, which is hard to correlate and insufficient for security reviews.

Behavior: - On successful verification, emit event bundle.verify.success. - On failure, emit bundle.verify.failure with reason_code mapped from the existing error taxonomy (e.g., HASH_MISMATCH, SIGNATURE_INVALID, UNKNOWN_KEY, CERT_NOT_YET_VALID, CERT_EXPIRED, MANIFEST_PARSE_ERROR, DOWNGRADE_BLOCKED, BUNDLE_LAYOUT_INVALID). - If break-glass override is used (introduced in a separate PR), emit bundle.verify.override_used with an explicit warning flag and the provided justification string (redacted by default).

Schema (v1): event_name, event_version=1, timestamp, installer_version, bundle_path (optional/redacted), bundle_build_id (if present in manifest), bundle_version, manifest_digest_sha256, signature_digest_sha256, signing_key_id (KID / fingerprint), trust_store_id, verification_mode (preflight|install), outcome (success|failure|override), reason_code (on failure), error_message (redacted by default), host_facts (optional: os, arch), and correlation_id.

Redaction: Added config keys to control the payload. Defaults are conservative for regulated installs: - redact.bundle_path=true (default) - redact.error_message=true (default; reason_code is preserved) - redact.justification=true (default) - include.host_facts=false (default) Operators can opt-in to more detail via installer config or CLI flags. The intent is to preserve a useful security signal without leaking filesystem layout, customer project names, or operator-entered text into audit logs.

Storage & retention: Events are written to the local audit sink as JSON lines at /var/lib/redwood-private-installer/audit/events.jsonl (path configurable). Rotation uses size-based rolling with max file count; defaults mirror our other local audit logs. Nothing is transmitted.

Support bundle: When support bundle generation is enabled, the last N verification events are included (subject to redaction settings) to help triage common failures like hash mismatches after offline transfer.

Testing: - Unit tests for schema generation, redaction behavior, and reason_code mapping. - Integration test updated to assert a failure produces a bundle.verify.failure event with the correct reason_code. - Golden event fixtures to keep schema stable.

Docs: Added a short section in installer docs: Where to find verification audit events, how to change redaction levels, and what fields are expected.

Release note: Private/on-prem installs now produce local audit events for offline bundle verification (success/failure) with configurable redaction.

Commits: 1) installer/audit: introduce VerificationEvent schema + sink plumbing 2) installer/verify: map verification errors to stable reason_code enum 3) installer/config: add redaction config + CLI flags 4) tests: add fixtures and schema regression tests 5) docs: describe local audit events and paths

Changed areas (high level): - pkg/verify/*: emit verification outcome events at key decision points - pkg/audit/*: local JSONL sink + rotation + support bundle hook - pkg/config/*: redaction and sink configuration - docs/*: audit/telemetry section

text:
Summary: This PR emits local audit/telemetry events during offline bundle signature+manifest verification. Events cover both success and failure paths with a stable schema and an explicit redaction policy suitable for air-gapped/regulated environments. The installer continues to make no outbound network calls; events are written locally and can later be exported via the existing audit-log-exporter integration (when present) or collected via support bundles.

Motivation: Support and customer security teams asked for an auditable record of verification outcomes to answer: (1) did the installer verify the bundle? (2) why did it fail? (3) which trust root was used? and (4) did anyone use override/break-glass. Prior behavior only printed CLI output and wrote unstructured logs, which is hard to correlate and insufficient for security reviews.

Behavior: - On successful verification, emit event bundle.verify.success. - On failure, emit bundle.verify.failure with reason_code mapped from the existing error taxonomy (e.g., HASH_MISMATCH, SIGNATURE_INVALID, UNKNOWN_KEY, CERT_NOT_YET_VALID, CERT_EXPIRED, MANIFEST_PARSE_ERROR, DOWNGRADE_BLOCKED, BUNDLE_LAYOUT_INVALID). - If break-glass override is used (introduced in a separate PR), emit bundle.verify.override_used with an explicit warning flag and the provided justification string (redacted by default).

Schema (v1): event_name, event_version=1, timestamp, installer_version, bundle_path (optional/redacted), bundle_build_id (if present in manifest), bundle_version, manifest_digest_sha256, signature_digest_sha256, signing_key_id (KID / fingerprint), trust_store_id, verification_mode (preflight|install), outcome (success|failure|override), reason_code (on failure), error_message (redacted by default), host_facts (optional: os, arch), and correlation_id.

Redaction: Added config keys to control the payload. Defaults are conservative for regulated installs: - redact.bundle_path=true (default) - redact.error_message=true (default; reason_code is preserved) - redact.justification=true (default) - include.host_facts=false (default) Operators can opt-in to more detail via installer config or CLI flags. The intent is to preserve a useful security signal without leaking filesystem layout, customer project names, or operator-entered text into audit logs.

Storage & retention: Events are written to the local audit sink as JSON lines at /var/lib/redwood-private-installer/audit/events.jsonl (path configurable). Rotation uses size-based rolling with max file count; defaults mirror our other local audit logs. Nothing is transmitted.

Support bundle: When support bundle generation is enabled, the last N verification events are included (subject to redaction settings) to help triage common failures like hash mismatches after offline transfer.

Testing: - Unit tests for schema generation, redaction behavior, and reason_code mapping. - Integration test updated to assert a failure produces a bundle.verify.failure event with the correct reason_code. - Golden event fixtures to keep schema stable.

Docs: Added a short section in installer docs: Where to find verification audit events, how to change redaction levels, and what fields are expected.

Release note: Private/on-prem installs now produce local audit events for offline bundle verification (success/failure) with configurable redaction.

title: Fix air-gapped time skew causing "certificate not yet valid" during bundle signature verification
preview:
Context: Several pilot/on-prem installs in fully air-gapped environments hit preflight failures when verifying signed offline bundles with errors like `x509: certificate has expired or is not yet valid` (typically "not yet valid"). Root cause was cluster/installer host clocks being behind (RTC drift, no NTP, staged VM templates). Because signature verification is time-bound (cert validity + signature timestamp), the verifier correctly failed closed, but the UX was misleading and the remediation path was unclear.

What this PR changes:
- Improves detection and messaging for the specific "not yet valid" time-skew case: we now surface the current system time, the signing cert `NotBefore` value, and the computed skew, plus a clear remediation checklist (set clock / enable NTP source / validate VM template time).
- Adds a small skew tolerance window (default 10 minutes) to account for minor clock drift without weakening the threat model for real replay/downgrade cases. This is intentionally small and only affects the `NotBefore` boundary check.
- Adds an explicit diagnostic-only override to run verification with a caller-provided time, so Support/SE can confirm "this is just time skew" without telling the customer to bypass verification. New flag: `--verify-time-override RFC3339` (preflight + inspect only). When used, we emit a local audit marker and print a warning that this does not change the required production behavior.
- Updates docs/help output for air-gapped environments with guidance on time sources (e.g., offline NTP appliances, hypervisor time sync, manual clock setting procedures) and common pitfalls.

Security stance:
- Default behavior remains fail-closed.
- The override is opt-in, diagnostic-only, and does not enable continuing install when verification fails for other reasons (unknown key, hash mismatch, expired cert, invalid signature). It is intended to help triage/support and to reduce "false incident" escalations.

Testing:
- Unit tests for parsing/validation of RFC3339 override values and skew calculations.
- Integration test coverage for the not-yet-valid path to ensure we provide actionable remediation steps and correct audit markers.

Follow-ups:
- The broader time-skew guidance is tracked under ENG-4850 (docs/runbooks).

title: Support detached signature layout (manifest.json + manifest.sig) for offline bundles
preview:
Context: As part of offline bundle signing for Redwood Private, we need a stable on-disk layout that works well with standard file transfer tooling (USB, secure courier, malware scanning gateways) and doesn’t require rewriting the manifest to “embed” signature metadata. This PR adds first-class support for detached signatures and documents the expected bundle layout.

What’s changing: The installer now accepts either (a) detached signature layout: <bundle>/manifest.json + <bundle>/manifest.sig, or (b) the legacy/early layout where the signature is embedded in a single envelope file (supported for backwards compatibility during pilot). The verification preflight resolves the signature file by convention (manifest.sig alongside manifest.json) and fails closed if a signature is required but missing/unreadable.

Bundle layout (new recommended): - bundle-root/ - manifest.json (canonical JSON) - manifest.sig (detached signature over canonical bytes of manifest.json) - artifacts/ (charts, images index, runtime/model artifacts, etc.) - sbom/ (optional, referenced by manifest)

Implementation notes: - Verification flow now loads manifest.json, canonicalizes it using the same rules as the signer (stable key ordering + UTF-8, no trailing whitespace), then verifies manifest.sig against those canonical bytes. - The trust store selection logic is unchanged; this PR only changes how the signature is located and the bytes it covers. - Error messages were updated to be explicit about expected paths (e.g. "expected signature file at <bundle>/manifest.sig"). - Added unit + integration tests for: missing signature file, swapped signature file, signature present but for different manifest bytes, and “both present” behavior (prefer detached signature if both embedded envelope and manifest.sig exist).

Why detached signatures: - Avoids mutating the manifest after it’s generated (less room for drift and easier deterministic builds). - Plays nicer with customers that require separate signature artifacts for scanning/attestation workflows. - Makes it easier to inspect bundles with common tooling (cat/grep/jq) without needing signature envelope parsing.

Security considerations: - Signature verification remains mandatory in air-gapped mode when signing is enabled (default for signed-bundle-capable installer builds). - We still fail closed on verification errors; break-glass behavior is not introduced here (tracked separately).

Docs: Included README updates for the offline bundle directory layout and examples of how Support/SE should describe the required files during bundle transfer validation.

Test plan: - unit: go test ./... - integration: make test-offline-bundle-verify (covers detached signature happy path + negative cases) - manual: generated a sample bundle from signing pipeline output, removed manifest.sig to confirm failure is clear/actionable.

Follow-ups: - Update the docs site to match the detached signature layout as the primary example (DevEx PR will reference this). - Align bundle generation tooling to always emit manifest.sig (some earlier pilot tooling still wrote the envelope file only).

title: Add signing trust store + basic key rollover support for offline bundles
preview: Context: Redwood Private offline bundles will be cryptographically signed and must be verifiable in air-gapped installs. We need a trust model that supports (a) multiple trusted signing keys (during rotation) and (b) a predictable way for customers to update trust roots in offline environments without rebuilding the installer. This PR introduces a local trust store mechanism and implements basic key rollover support in the bundle verification path. Summary of changes: (1) Adds a trust store file format (JSON) that can contain multiple trusted public keys, each with a stable key_id (kid), algorithm, and optional metadata (created_at, not_before, not_after, comment). (2) Installer can load trust store keys from: default embedded trust store (compiled into the binary) OR an operator-provided trust store path (air-gapped friendly) OR an OS-level path (/etc/redwood-private/trust/signing-truststore.json) if present. Precedence: explicit CLI flag > env var > OS path > embedded. (3) Signature verification now selects the verification key by kid when present in the signature envelope; if kid is absent (migration case), we attempt verification against all trusted keys and require exactly one match (fail closed if 0 or >1 match). (4) Key rollover behavior: trust store supports multiple active keys simultaneously. Installer logs which kid was used, and emits a warning if the matched key is marked as "deprecated" in the trust store metadata (non-blocking; intended to help customers notice rotation lag). (5) Adds clear failure modes and remediation hints: unknown kid, empty trust store, malformed key material, and "multiple keys verify" ambiguity (points to duplicated keys / incorrect trust store content). (6) Adds unit tests covering trust store parsing, precedence rules, kid-based selection, and multi-key verification behavior. Notes / non-goals: This PR does not implement key revocation distribution, certificate chains, or customer-managed custom roots (future work tracked separately). It also intentionally does not add network fetches for trust material; offline installs must not depend on outbound connectivity. Follow-ups: docs PR to describe how to update the trust store in air-gapped environments and how rotation will be communicated; integration tests for tampered trust store file will land with the broader verification suite. Risk: low/medium. Changes are gated behind the offline bundle verification code path and default behavior remains "embedded trust store only" unless an operator configures overrides. Tested: unit tests; manual verification with two generated ed25519 keys and a signed manifest; confirmed fail-closed on unknown kid and on empty trust store.

title: Add `bundle inspect` command for offline bundle diagnostics
preview:
Problem
Support/SE and on-prem operators currently have to run a full preflight (or hand-parse the bundle) to answer basic questions like: “What version is this bundle?”, “Is the manifest present?”, “Which signing key signed it?”, and “Did the offline transfer corrupt anything?”. For air-gapped environments, we need an offline-first diagnostic command that can be run on a laptop/jump host without starting an install.

What this PR does
Adds a new CLI subcommand: `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect <bundle-path>`.
The command is designed to be safe to run on a customer machine and produces a concise report that can be pasted into a Support ticket.

Capabilities
- Locate and load the bundle manifest (current: `bundle.manifest.json`; supports the existing legacy name `manifest.json` for pilot bundles).
- Validate manifest schema (required fields present; version fields parse; artifact entries have name/path/hash).
- Print versions/build identifiers (bundle version, build ID, chart appVersions, runtime/model artifact versions when present).
- Verify signature when present (reads signature sidecar `bundle.manifest.sig` when available; otherwise reports “unsigned” with guidance).
- Verify trust chain using the installer trust store by default; optionally allow `--trust-store <path>` for air-gapped key rollouts.
- Compute and compare sha256 for each artifact entry (streaming) to catch corruption after transfer.

CLI usage
- `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect ./redwood-private-bundle-2026.01.0.tgz`
- `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect ./bundle/ --json`
- `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect ./bundle/ --no-hash` (fast path for very large bundles)
- `redwood-private-installer bundle inspect ./bundle/ --trust-store ./trust-store.pem`

Example output (text)
Bundle: ./redwood-private-bundle-2026.01.0.tgz
Manifest: OK (schema v1)
Bundle version: 2026.01.0  Build ID: priv-2026.01.0+build.4121
Signer: key_id=RWDSIGN-2025Q4-01  status=TRUSTED  signature=VALID
Artifacts: 37 verified (sha256)
Charts: redwood-core=2026.01.0 redwood-observability=2026.01.0
Notes: No network access required. For tamper/corruption failures, re-transfer bundle and re-run with `--json` for Support.

Output format
- Default: human-readable, stable labels intended for copy/paste into tickets.
- `--json`: machine-readable output (intended for future “support bundle” integration), includes error_code and failure_reason when verification fails.

Failure modes / error taxonomy
The inspect command intentionally mirrors the preflight error taxonomy where possible.
- Missing manifest: exits non-zero with `BUNDLE_MANIFEST_MISSING`
- Manifest parse/validation error: `BUNDLE_MANIFEST_INVALID`
- Signature missing: exit code remains 0 (informational) unless `--require-signature` is set
- Signature invalid/untrusted: non-zero with `BUNDLE_SIGNATURE_INVALID` or `BUNDLE_SIGNING_KEY_UNTRUSTED`
- Hash mismatch: non-zero with `BUNDLE_ARTIFACT_HASH_MISMATCH` and the offending path(s)

Security / privacy considerations
- No file contents are printed; only paths, sizes (optional), hashes, and version metadata.
- JSON output redacts absolute paths by default when the bundle is a directory (uses relative paths from bundle root).
- Does not attempt any outbound network calls (explicitly enforced by using only local filesystem readers).

Tests
- Golden tests for: valid signed bundle, unsigned bundle, missing manifest, invalid JSON, hash mismatch.
- Regression test for Windows path separators and spaces (pilot environments).

Docs
Adds a short CLI reference section under installer docs with troubleshooting examples for Support/SE.

Rollout
This is additive and safe. Support can start recommending `bundle inspect` immediately for air-gapped cases; preflight behavior is unchanged.

Related
- ENG-4826 (support tooling)
- PR #24844 (preflight signature verification)
- PR #24831 (manifest introduced)

Screenshots/logs
Included in comments below: sample output for both text and `--json`.

Checklist
- [x] Help text + examples
- [x] Unit tests + fixtures
- [x] Lint/format
- [x] Docs snippet

title: automated-az-switchover-playbooks-terraform-helm
preview: Motivation: automate multi-AZ switchover for Private deployments and encode DR runbooks as code so runbooks are executable, testable, and versioned. This PR introduces: 1) a Terraform module that wires up cross-AZ routing and health-check failover for Private clusters in customer VPCs; 2) Helm chart hooks and readiness probes to ensure stateful services (controller, kv-store) drain cleanly before leader evacuation; 3) Ansible/Playbook-driven 'switchover' runbooks (playbooks-as-code) for operators to execute or run during scheduled drills; 4) a safe rollback utility and CI e2e drill job to validate the full failover and restore path. Scope: supports AWS multi-AZ (subnet-awareness), mechanisms for on-prem inventory mapping (rack/az tags), and a new 'drill-runner' CI job that will run synthetic traffic against canary replicas and validate KV sync and stateful reattach. Implementation notes: - terraform/modules/az-switchover: new module exposing variables for route53/elb-weighting, cross-AZ peering, and optional traffic-shift policies. - helm/redwood-private: added postStart/preStop hooks, new liveness/readiness templates, and values to control drain windows. - installer/playbooks/az_switchover.yml: idempotent playbook that performs detection, cordon, drain, shift routes, and verification steps; includes a --dry-run mode. - tools/drill-validate.sh: end-to-end validation runner used by CI and SRE during scheduled drills. - docs/drill-playbooks.md: operational runbook and decision tree generated from the playbooks (runbooks-as-code concept). Checklist: [x] Terraform module (initial implementation) [x] Helm hooks + templates [x] Playbook + dry-run support [x] CI e2e drill job added [x] Documentation and runbook generated [x] Tests: unit + e2e drills (observability validated) Backout plan: built into playbook; explicit rollback path documented and automated via terraform destroy/replicate previous routing weights to 100%. See 'backout_plan' field for high-level steps. Migration note: existing private clusters without AZ tags will continue to function; module is opt-in via installer flag. Related: references ENG-1723 and SRE-88 for on-call runbook alignment.

title: Add canonical offline bundle manifest (hashes + artifact inventory) and wire into bundle build
preview:
Context: Air-gapped / offline installs currently rely on “whatever is in the tarball” plus best-effort checksums produced by ad-hoc scripts. For offline signing + verification (follow-up PRs), the installer needs a deterministic, canonical manifest that enumerates bundle contents and pins hashes/versions. This PR introduces a v0 manifest file at the root of each offline bundle, updates the bundle generation tooling to produce it deterministically, and adds unit/integration tests to prevent regressions.

What’s included:
- New file in bundle root: redwood-bundle-manifest.json (canonical JSON; stable key ordering and artifact list ordering)
- Manifest schema v0.1:
  - schema_version, created_at_utc, bundle_id (build id), redwood_private_version, installer_min_version
  - hash_algorithm (sha256)
  - artifacts[] inventory with: path, kind (image-tar|helm-chart|binary|config|model-artifact|sbom|other), sha256, size_bytes, logical_name, version (optional), source (optional)
  - top-level bundle_sha256_over_artifact_hashes (sha256 over canonical concatenation of artifact entries; used for quick consistency checks)
- Deterministic build behavior:
  - Sort artifacts by path (byte-order) and normalize path separators to “/” in the manifest
  - Stream hashing for large artifacts to avoid loading into memory
  - Fail build if any artifact is missing or duplicate paths are detected
- Installer/tooling updates:
  - bundle build command now always emits the manifest and prints its path
  - “--offline” mode now asserts manifest presence (but does not yet enforce signature verification; that lands in PR 24844)
- Tests:
  - Golden manifest test for a small fixture bundle
  - Regression test ensuring stable output across runs (no non-deterministic ordering)
  - Tamper test: modifying a file changes its sha256 and causes manifest verification helper to fail

Compatibility:
- This PR does not change install semantics for existing unsigned bundles *yet*. The installer warns when a bundle is missing the manifest (to unblock incremental rollout), but bundle generation in our pipeline will start producing manifests immediately after merge.

Notes / follow-ups:
- Signing and verification will be layered on top of this manifest (detached signature over canonical manifest bytes).
- SBOM pointer + hash will be added once the SBOM artifact location is finalized (tracked separately).

Screenshots / examples:
- Example root layout:
  - /redwood-bundle-manifest.json
  - /images/*.tar
  - /charts/*.tgz
  - /manifests/*.yaml
  - /bin/redwood-private-installer

- Example manifest excerpt:
  - artifacts[0].path: "images/redwood-runtime_2025.11.0.tar"
  - artifacts[0].sha256: "<sha256>"
  - artifacts[0].kind: "image-tar"
